chunks.text: Thyroid disorders occur when the thyroid gland produces too much (hyperthyroidism) or too little (hypothyroidism) hormone, disrupting the body's metabolic rate.
An estimated 200 million people worldwide have some form of thyroid disease. Women are five to eight times more likely than men to experience thyroid dysfunction.
Hypothyroidism causes fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance. Hyperthyroidism presents as rapid heart rate, weight loss, anxiety, and heat sensitivity.
Autoimmune conditions like Hashimoto's (low) or Graves' (high) are primary causes. Iodine deficiency remains a significant cause in developing regions, while nodules or inflammation are also common.
Diagnosis is confirmed via blood tests measuring Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and Free T4 levels. Ultrasound is used to evaluate physical nodules.
Hypothyroidism is managed with daily synthetic hormone (Levothyroxine). Hyperthyroidism treatments include anti-thyroid medications, radioactive iodine, or surgery.
While many cases are autoimmune, ensuring adequate dietary iodine intake and avoiding excessive radiation exposure to the neck area are standard protective measures.
